title: Architect Rounds
description: Architecture tradeoffs, platform strategy, reliability, observability, governance, vendor selection, and cost.

questions.title: At architecture level, how do you decide between RAG and fine-tuning?
questions.scenario: A CTO asks for one strategy across support, legal, and engineering assistants.
questions.expectedAnswer: RAG is for grounding answers in changing or access-controlled knowledge. Fine-tuning is for behavior, style, domain adaptation, or task performance. Architects should consider data freshness, governance, cost, latency, evaluation, and whether a hybrid approach is needed. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A CTO asks for one strategy across support, legal, and engineering assistants. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by mapping the concept to a concrete workflow, choosing the simplest suitable technique, validating it with data or user feedback, and explaining how it changes the product or platform behavior.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "At architecture level, how do you decide between RAG and fine-tuning?", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real architect rounds setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Postgres vs Redis for AI memory: how do you choose?
questions.scenario: An assistant needs both session context and long-term user preferences.
questions.expectedAnswer: Postgres is durable, relational, queryable, and strong for governed long-term memory. Redis is fast and useful for ephemeral session state, caches, and short-lived memory. A strong answer discusses consistency, retention, search needs, scaling, cost, and compliance. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An assistant needs both session context and long-term user preferences. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: SSE vs WebSockets for streaming LLM output?
questions.scenario: A web assistant streams model tokens and occasionally needs client-side interruption.
questions.expectedAnswer: SSE is simpler and works well for one-way server-to-client token streaming. WebSockets support bidirectional communication and richer interactive protocols. Choose based on interaction pattern, infrastructure support, reconnection, scale, and operational complexity. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A web assistant streams model tokens and occasionally needs client-side interruption. questions.title: How do you design reliable GenAI systems?
questions.scenario: A provider outage breaks several internal assistants.
questions.expectedAnswer: Reliability requires retries, fallbacks, circuit breakers, rate limits, provider failover, timeouts, graceful degradation, caching, evaluation gates, monitoring, and incident response. Architects should define SLOs and distinguish model errors from system errors. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A provider outage breaks several internal assistants. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for architect rounds.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "How do you design reliable GenAI systems?", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real architect rounds setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: How do you evaluate GenAI vendors?
questions.scenario: Procurement asks how to choose between model providers and AI platforms.
questions.expectedAnswer: Evaluate quality on your tasks, security, compliance, data usage terms, latency, uptime, roadmap, pricing, integration fit, observability, support, lock-in risk, and exit strategy. Strong answers include proof-of-concept evaluation and contractual governance. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.
